turns.text: Hello Leida, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I came into labour expecting more than clinical competence — I wanted someone who could read more than my monitor strips. I imagined a midwife who knew my history, who would sit with me, speak calmly through each contraction, hold my hand, remind me to breathe, and reassure me when my brain raced through worst‑case scenarios. I needed emotional steadiness as much as medical judgment.

The reality was mixed. There were moments of real kindness — one midwife stayed beside me, kept eye contact, and listened when I confessed how afraid I was. That held me together. But too often care felt transactional: staff focused on vitals and tasks, conversations were rushed or happened over my head, and I had to repeat details about my pre‑eclampsia and preferences as if they were new. Shift changes made continuity vanish. When I asked for small comforts — a hand to squeeze, a plain explanation of a decision — it sometimes took too long to get them. In the end, those human moments mattered more than I expected. When they were absent, I felt very alone.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I keep circling back to the small, concrete things that made this feel real — the way the hospital gown rustled, the fluorescent light that never seemed to dim, my partner’s hand cold and steady in mine. I expected care to be efficient and kind; what I needed was permission to be scared. When that permission came — a midwife who acknowledged my fear instead of smoothing it away — it changed everything, even for a moment.

Afterwards, the reality of postpartum has been quieter and more complex than I imagined. Physically I’m monitoring my blood pressure, watching for signs of pre‑eclampsia like a hawk, counting meds and appointments. Emotionally, I swing between overwhelming love and a numbness that worries me. My IVF background makes me hypervigilant; I ask for lab numbers, copies of notes, and explanations in plain language because uncertainty fuels my anxiety.

What would make care better? More continuity, honest conversations about worst‑case and most‑likely scenarios, real time-sharing of results, and follow‑up that treats the postpartum period as the fragile transition it is. I want to feel witnessed, not just checked off a list.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

Yes — it swung both ways, and the difference in how I felt was striking. I remember one antenatal visit with a midwife who pulled up my chart, asked how I felt about induction options given my blood pressure trends, talked through risks and benefits, and actually asked what I wanted. She wrote my preferences into the notes and checked back later. In that moment I felt like a partner in the plan.

By contrast, there were other times when decisions landed like edicts. The first time we crossed the threshold for magnesium therapy, the team convened and then told me the plan as if it were inevitable. I was given a consent form to sign with very little space to process or ask the specific questions my IVF background made urgent — dosing, monitoring, implications for labor. During shift changes I’d be told a new course and have to re‑explain my pre‑eclampsia history yet again. That made me anxious and sidelined.

What helps me is being explicitly invited into decisions, given time and clear numbers, and having those conversations recorded so I don’t have to repeat my story every few hours.

I keep thinking about how much the little things shaped my sense of safety. When clinicians actually asked me what I wanted and then wrote it down, I felt steadier — like I had a seat at the table instead of being an emergency variable. That contrasted sharply with the times decisions were presented as finished and I was expected to comply. Those moments made my chest tighten; I’d replay conversations later thinking, “Did I say that? Did they hear me?”

Because of my IVF background, numbers and rationale are how I anchor myself. If someone says “we’ll monitor,” I want to know how often, what thresholds, and who will call me if things change. Not for control, but so the unknown isn’t an enemy. I also needed better handovers — shift changes felt like being passed between strangers. A printed plan or a quick in-room huddle helped when it happened.

Postpartum follow-up has been a weak spot: clearer BP check schedules, a defined person to call about concerns, and emotional support or screening for postpartum anxiety would have made a big difference. I want care that recognizes fear isn’t a failure — it’s information, and it deserves space.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?
